food_name: Char Koay Kak (Penang Stir-Fried Rice Cake)

allergens: gluten (dark soy sauce may contain wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of char koay kak weighs approximately 280g, comprising roughly 220g of steamed rice and tapioca flour cake cubes, 2 eggs (~100g), 15g chai poh (preserved radish), bean sprouts, chili, and dark soy sauce.

nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for char koay kak / fried turnip cake. Per-100g values are as reported. MyFCD is an approved primary regional source for Malaysian dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based and Starchy Composite Dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed white rice (GI ~72) and mixed stir-fried rice dishes (GI ~60–68)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for char koay kak in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~65 is estimated from the closest analogous foods: steamed white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) and mixed stir-fried rice-based dishes (GI ~60–68). The rice and tapioca flour cake base drives the GI upward; eggs, lard/oil, and dark soy sauce caramelisation moderately reduce the effective GI. GL per 280g plate = (65 × 53.2g carbohydrate) / 100 = 34.6 (HIGH). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1,344mg per 280g plate (480mg/100g), driven by chai poh (preserved salted radish ~3,200mg Na/100g) and dark soy sauce (~400mg Na per 2 tbsp); exceeds 58% of the 2,300mg daily sodium limit in a single serving.
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit char koay kak to occasional consumption; at home, rinse chai poh 2–3 times in cold water and squeeze dry before use to reduce chai poh sodium by ~25–30%, and substitute low-sodium soy sauce for dark soy to bring total plate sodium down to approximately 800–900mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load — GL ~34.6 per 280g plate (HIGH), driven by the rice and tapioca flour cake base (GI ~65) delivering ~53.2g available carbohydrate per serving; egg and fat content provide only modest glycaemic moderation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid a full plate of char koay kak; a half portion (~140g, GL ~17) is more manageable, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and protein can further blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.2g/100g, ~6.2g per plate) from lard at traditional stalls, combined with high sodium (1,344mg/plate) and dietary cholesterol (~182mg per plate from 2 eggs and lard); the sodium load is the dominant cardiovascular risk factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request vegetable oil instead of lard when possible, limit portion size to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, and consume char koay kak no more than once per week within an otherwise heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium load (1,344mg/plate) may exacerbate inflammation; lard-based preparation contributes saturated fat; however, egg vitamin D (~1.4mcg per plate) and potential probiotic content of lacto-fermented chai poh may offer minor anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should be aware of the high sodium content and request oil-based preparation; occasional consumption within a low-sodium, anti-inflammatory dietary pattern is acceptable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char Koay Kak is a distinctly Penang version of the Southern Chinese (Teochew/Hokkien) stir-fried rice cake tradition, widely regarded as one of Penang's most iconic hawker dishes. The dish is built around 'koay kak' — soft, gelatinous cubes made from a steamed rice flour and tapioca flour batter — which are wok-fried at high heat with 2 eggs (scrambled directly in the wok), chai poh (preserved salted radish), fresh bean sprouts, chili, and dark soy sauce. Traditional stalls use lard for wok-frying, which imparts a characteristic smoky, caramelised flavour; modern stalls may substitute vegetable oil. The result is a dish with a slightly crispy exterior on the cake cubes, soft interior, savoury-sweet dark soy caramelisation, and a rich egg coating. A standard hawker plate weighs approximately 280g and delivers ~414 kcal, 12.6g protein, 16.8g fat, and 53.2g carbohydrate. Sodium is very high at ~1,344mg per plate, dominated by chai poh (~3,200mg Na/100g) and dark soy sauce. The glycemic load is HIGH (~34.6 per plate, GI ~65), making this dish unsuitable for uncontrolled Type 2 Diabetes. Char koay kak is NOT Halal at traditional lard-using stalls. It is dairy-free. The dish is distinct from Singapore's Chai Tow Kway (which uses a daikon/radish-primary cake); Penang Char Koay Kak uses a rice flour + tapioca flour base producing a softer, more gelatinous texture. Nutrient data sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from IMR Malaysia MyFCD, an approved primary regional source for Malaysian dishes. The MyFCD entry for char koay kak / fried turnip cake provides per-100g values for all core macros and a comprehensive micronutrient panel. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) from University of Sydney GI Database comparators. All values are internally consistent with the described dish composition.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for char koay kak not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the MyFCD entry for this dish. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per MyFCD source). All other micronutrients are as reported by MyFCD. GI ~65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed white rice (GI ~72) and mixed stir-fried rice-based dishes (GI ~60–68); no direct GI measurement for char koay kak exists in the database. GL per 280g plate = (65 × 53.2) / 100 = 34.6 (HIGH). Lard is used at traditional Penang hawker stalls — this dish is NOT Halal at such stalls. Chai poh sodium is approximately 3,200mg Na/100g; rinsing before use reduces sodium by ~25–30%. The Penang version of this dish uses a rice flour + tapioca flour cake base (not primarily daikon/radish as in Singapore Chai Tow Kway). Tamil and Hindi local names are not established for this Penang Chinese hawker dish and have been left null; this dish is not a Singaporean dish and the Singaporean multilingual requirement (Tamil, Hindi) does not apply — cuisine is Malaysian Penang Chinese.
